title: Planning
summary: Planning is deciding in advance what to do, how to do, when to do, and who is to do it. It bridges the gap between where we are and where we want to go.
Planning is the first and foremost function of management — all other functions follow from it.
Features: Goal-oriented, Primary function, Pervasive, Continuous, Futuristic, Involves decision-making, Mental exercise.
Importance: Provides direction, Reduces risk of uncertainty, Reduces overlapping/wasteful activities, Promotes innovative ideas, Facilitates decision-making, Establishes standards for controlling.
Limitations: Rigidity, May not work in dynamic environment, Reduces creativity, Involves huge costs, Time-consuming, Does not guarantee success.
Planning process: Setting objectives → Developing premises → Identifying alternatives → Evaluating alternatives → Selecting best alternative → Implementing → Follow-up.
Types of Plans: Standing Plans (Objectives, Strategy, Policy, Procedure, Method, Rule) and Single-Use Plans (Programme, Budget).

teacher_tip.title: AKSpected Shortcut
teacher_tip.text: If asked 'Planning & Controlling are inseparable twins' — explain that planning sets standards, controlling measures performance against those standards. Without planning, controlling has no basis; without controlling, planning has no follow-up. They are both backward-looking and forward-looking.

sections.title: Meaning of Planning
sections.content: Planning is the function of determining in advance what to do and how to do it. It involves setting objectives and developing an appropriate course of action to achieve those objectives.

definition.text: Deciding in advance what to do, how to do it, when to do it, and who is to do it. It bridges the gap from where we are to where we want to go.

sections.title: Features of Planning
points: 1. Goal-Oriented: Planning is purposeful — done to achieve specific objectives.
2. Primary Function: It precedes all other functions — organising, staffing, directing, and controlling follow planning.
3. Pervasive: Required at all levels — top (plans for whole organisation), middle (departmental plans), lower (plans for execution).
4. Continuous: Plans are made, executed, and followed by new plans — it never stops.
5. Futuristic: Planning looks ahead — it's about the future, not the past.
6. Involves Decision-Making: Choosing from alternatives — if there's only one option, no planning is needed.
7. Mental Exercise: Requires thinking, analysing, and creativity — not physical activity.

sections.title: Importance of Planning
points: 1. Provides Direction: States in advance what and how to do — both employees and managers know their targets.
2. Reduces Risk of Uncertainty: Anticipates future changes and prepares to face them.
3. Reduces Overlapping: Eliminates useless and redundant activities, ensuring clarity.
4. Promotes Innovation: Forces managers to think of new ideas and approaches.
5. Facilitates Decision-Making: Provides criteria for evaluating alternatives.
6. Establishes Standards for Controlling: Plans set the benchmarks against which actual performance is measured.

sections.title: Limitations of Planning
points: 1. Rigidity: Following a pre-decided plan may restrict flexibility.
2. Dynamic Environment: Plans may become irrelevant due to rapid environmental changes.
3. Reduces Creativity: Middle and lower levels just follow plans — no scope for own thinking.
4. Huge Costs: Scientific planning involves research, forecasting, and expertise — all costly.
5. Time-Consuming: Collecting data, analysing, and formulating plans takes time.
6. No Guarantee of Success: Future is uncertain — even the best plans may fail.

sections.title: Types of Plans
points: Objectives: The end points that management seeks to achieve. Must be measurable.
Strategy: Comprehensive plan to achieve objectives, considering competitive environment.
Policy: General statement that guides decision-making. Example: 'We promote from within.'
Procedure: Routine steps to carry out activities in chronological order.
Method: Prescribed way of performing a specific task — even more detailed than procedure.
Rule: Specific statement that tells what is/isn't to be done. No flexibility. Example: 'No smoking.'
Programme: Detailed plan for a specific project — includes objectives, policies, procedures, rules, budgets, and time schedule.
Budget: A plan in numerical terms expressing expected results — statements of expected revenue and expenditure.

tables.title: Standing Plans vs Single-Use Plans
tables.tip: Know at least 3 types of each for board exam.
rows: Objectives, Strategy, Policy, Procedure, Method, Rule

questions.text: 'No smoking in the office' is an example of:
questions.answer: Rule. A rule is a specific statement that tells what must or must not be done. No flexibility.

questions.text: State any three limitations of planning.
questions.answer:
(i) Rigidity: Following pre-decided plans restricts flexibility.
(ii) Dynamic Environment: Plans may become irrelevant due to rapid changes.
(iii) No Guarantee of Success: Future is uncertain — even the best plans may fail.

questions.text: Distinguish between Policy and Rule with examples.
questions.answer:
Policy: General guideline for decision-making, allows some flexibility. Example: 'We promote from within.'
Rule: Specific statement with no flexibility. Example: 'No smoking in the office.'
A policy guides thinking; a rule demands specific action.

questions.text:
TechSpark set a sales target (Planning), structured teams and resources (Organising), hired sales personnel (Staffing), held weekly motivational huddles (Directing), and tracked KPIs to tweak strategies (Controlling).

Identify which function is demonstrated by weekly motivational huddles and explain how KPI tracking relates to controlling.
questions.answer:
1. Directing: Weekly motivational huddles involve leading, influencing, and motivating employees — core elements of directing.

2. Controlling: KPI tracking monitors actual performance against planned standards. When strategies are tweaked based on deviations, it completes the controlling process — ensuring organisational goals are achieved.

pyqs.text: Explain any two features of planning.
pyqs.answer:
(i) Goal-Oriented: Planning exists to achieve specific objectives — it is purposeful.
(ii) Futuristic: Planning looks ahead and anticipates future conditions to prepare accordingly.

pyqs.text: Explain the relationship between planning and controlling.
pyqs.answer: Planning and controlling are inseparable twins. Planning provides the standards; controlling measures actual performance against those standards. Without planning, there's no basis for controlling. Without controlling, planning is just wishful thinking. Planning is looking ahead; controlling is looking back. Together they form a continuous cycle of improvement.

related_chapters.title: Nature of Management
related_chapters.reason: Planning is the first function of management

related_chapters.title: Controlling
related_chapters.reason: Planning and Controlling are inseparable twins

mnemonics.title: Types of Plans — SOP-PMR + PB
mnemonics.text:
Standing: Strategy, Objectives, Policy, Procedure, Method, Rule
Single-use: Programme, Budget
